content.problemStatement:
You are given a string SSS of length NNN.

The string contains only lowercase English letters.

You can freely rearrange the characters of SSS however you like.

After rearrangement, is it possible to make the first and last characters of SSS equal?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of string SSS.
The second line contains the string SSS of length NNN.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: either Yes\tt{Yes}Yes or No\tt{No}No, depending on whether it's possible to rearrange the string appropriately or not.


Each character of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, for example the strings yes,YES,yES\tt{yes}, \tt{YES}, \tt{yES}yes,YES,yES will all be accepted if valid rearrangements exist.
constraints: SSS contains only lowercase English letters, i.e. Si∈{a,b,…,z}S_i \in \{\tt{a,b,}\ldots, \tt{z}\}Si​∈{a,b,…,z}.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: S=akaS = \tt{aka}S=aka, so its first and last characters are already equal.
Test case 444: S=foggyS = \tt{foggy}S=foggy, and it can be rearranged to obtain S=gyfogS = \tt{gyfog}S=gyfog which has equal first and last characters.
In test cases 222 and 333, it can be proved that no rearrangement of the given string has equal first and last characters.
